sections.title: 1. Auxiliary Verbs: Sein (To Be) & Haben (To Have)
sections.description: In German, these are the most fundamental verbs. Sein is used for identity, state, and age, while Haben is used for possession.

rows: Ich bin Lehrer. (I am a teacher.)
Hast du Geld? (Do you have money?)
Er ist 5 Jahre alt. (He is 5 years old.)
Wir haben einen Hund. (We have a dog.)
Seid ihr neu hier? (Are you all new here?)

sections.title: 2. Special Verbs (Conjugation)
sections.description: These verbs are "special" because they follow unique patterns, such as vowel changes in the stem or identical forms for ich and er/sie/es.

content.note: Note for Learners: In many special or modal-like verbs, the 1st person (ich) and 3rd person (er/sie/es) forms are identical (e.g., Ich mag / Er mag).

sections.title: 3. Detailed Usage & Meaning
sections.description: Learn the specific meanings and usage contexts of these special verbs.

examples.meaning: Used for polite requests or orders in restaurants/shops.
examples.example: Wir möchten etwas trinken. (We would like something to drink.)

examples.meaning: Used to express a general preference for objects, people, or places.
Used for knowing facts or pieces of information.
examples.example: Ich weiß das nicht. (I don't know that.)

examples.meaning: Besides meaning "to do," it is commonly used with "weh" to express physical pain.
examples.example: Meine Ohren tun weh. (My ears hurt.)

sections.title: 4. Practice Sentences & Translations
sections.description: Practice using these verbs in real sentences with translations.

examples.german: Weißt du, wo der Bahnhof ist?
examples.english: Do you know where the train station is?
Do very little. (Take it easy / Work less)
What hurts? / Where is the pain?
